summary_text:
Chief Complaint
The patient reports vision changes and difficulty with reading, particularly in dim light.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports vision changes, particularly difficulty reading in dim light, and reports that straight lines appear distorted. He has attempted self-management strategies including hypnosis, lozenges, and chewing gum, but has struggled to quit smoking and has inconsistently taken his blood pressure medication. He reports a history of needle stick to the eye.

Findings
The patient's anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes. The right eye cornea is clear with no detectable nuclear vortex or cup-to-disc ratio of greater than 0.2. The left eye cornea is clear with no detectable nuclear vortex or cup-to-disc ratio of greater than 0.2. Fundus examination of the right eye reveals the retina attached at 360 degrees, with no evidence of retinal holes or tears, and mild retinal pigment epithelium changes in the fovea. The posterior segment examination reveals drusen in the macula, retinal thickening, and subretinal fluid and exudate accumulation, consistent with a hemorrhage. The left eye shows retinal attachment at 360 degrees, mild retinal pigment epithelium changes in the fovea, and no hemorrhage or subretinal fluid. Diagnostic testing reveals drusen deposits under the retinal pigment epithelium and subretinal fluid accumulation. Fluorescein angiography confirms focal leakage in the right eye consistent with neurovascular age-related macular degeneration.

Assessment
The patient is diagnosed with neurovascular age-related macular degeneration, advanced to a stage that typically leads to more severe vision loss.

Plan
A monthly injection of lucent will be administered into the vitreous to slow the progression of vision loss. The nurse will prepare the patient for the injection today, and a follow-up appointment will be scheduled. The patient should continue to take his blood pressure medication as prescribed and monitor his blood pressure regularly. A physical exam of the eyes will be performed before scheduling the injection.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Vision changes in the right eye.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient reports history of high blood pressure.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient reports he is smoking 1 pack of cigarettes daily.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Eyes: Reports right eye vision changes.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Eyes
- Examination of the right eye: Posterior chamber intraocular lenses present bilaterally. Right eye cornea is clear without NVI and cup-disc ratio is 0.2. Fundus of right eye shows retina is intact 360 degrees without holes or tears noted, and RPE changes in fovea. Posterior segment shows drusen of the macula, retinal thickening, and presence of subretinal fluid and exudate accumulation. Positive hemorrhage
Examination of the left eye: Cornea is clear. No NVI. Cup-disc ration is 0.3. RPE changes in fovea. No hemorrhage noted or subretinal fluid noted. Retina is attached appropriately.

RESULTS

OTC:
Drusen deposits under the RPE. SRF accumulation and choroidal neovascular membrane can also be visualized.

Fluorescein angiography:
Reveals that the focal area of the leakage in the right eye is consistent with neovascular age related macular degeneration.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Wet macular degeneration.
- Medical Reasoning: The diagnostic test and eye exams are consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, also called wet macular degeneration.
- Patient Education and Counseling: I explained the nature of the diagnosis and that this is advanced to a stage that usually leads to more severe vision loss happening. We discussed this happens when those abnormal blood vessels start to grow beneath the retina leaking fluid and blood. Secondary to that, it creates a large blind spot in the center of his visual field. He was advised that this is the most common cause of severe vision loss. I explained the treatment plan at length. He understands he is going to be injected in the vitreous portion of the eye after we numb the eye, so he is not going to feel any pain. I advised injections given over time can slow the rate of the vision decline and maybe even stop it. However, I also have to let him know that there could be a situation where he may still note a small amount of vision loss, but this is his best chance of maintaining that vision for as long as he can. Additionally, patient was counseled on the importance of taking his blood pressure medication. Questions were invited and answered today.
- Medical Treatment: Lucentis injections once a month with his first being performed today.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: A physical exam of the eyes will be performed before scheduling the injection.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The actual eye exam findings appropriately belong in Findings, but here the sentence is describing a future action (that an exam will be performed) as part of the care plan. This is acceptable as a plan item, though the phrasing blurs exam content vs. orders, so it is a minor organizational issue rather than a clear misplacement.
